text.title: rag Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for rag. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use chunk_size=512 for Q&A docs, 1024 for technical manuals, and 1500 for research papers. Use 20% overlap between chunks.
rule.rationale: Chunk size must balance retrieval precision (smaller = more targeted) with context completeness (larger = fewer split concepts). Document type determines the natural unit of meaning.

rule.statement: Test chunk size empirically on a representative sample of queries before committing to production parameters.
rule.rationale: Optimal chunk size is corpus-dependent. The defaults above are starting points; retrieval quality should be measured on the actual query distribution.

rule.statement: Add metadata at index time: source filename, page number, section heading, and ingestion date. This cannot be added retroactively without re-indexing.
rule.rationale: Metadata enables filtering, citation generation, and staleness detection. Adding it after indexing requires full re-embedding of all documents, which is expensive and causes downtime.

rule.statement: Remove near-duplicate chunks before indexing (cosine similarity greater than 0.97). Duplicate chunks waste storage and bias retrieval toward repeated content.
rule.rationale: Duplicate chunks appear multiple times in top-k results, crowding out diverse relevant content and inflating context with redundant text.

rule.statement: Implement incremental indexing: track document hashes, re-index only changed documents.
rule.rationale: Full re-indexing on every update is expensive at scale. Document hash comparison identifies changed documents in O(n) and limits embedding API cost to the changed subset.

rule.statement: Use hybrid search (BM25 + vector) by default. Fall back to pure vector only after benchmarking shows no improvement.
rule.rationale: Pure semantic search misses exact technical terms, product codes, and rare names. Hybrid search adds BM25 coverage with minimal additional latency on most vector databases.

rule.statement: Use a cross-encoder reranker for all production retrieval. Retrieve top-20, rerank to top-5.
rule.rationale: Vector search similarity scores are calibrated for recall, not precision. A cross-encoder reranker scores candidate chunks against the full query, improving precision at the cost of 100–300ms latency.

rule.statement: Ensure top_k * avg_chunk_tokens stays well below the model's context limit after adding system prompt and few-shot examples.
rule.rationale: Context overflow causes the model to drop earlier chunks. The dropped chunks are typically the less similar ones, but they may contain the answer for complex queries.

rule.statement: Cache frequent queries at the application layer — same query string produces the same retrieval result.
rule.rationale: Embedding + retrieval adds 200–600ms. For repeated queries in high-traffic applications, caching eliminates this latency entirely.

rule.statement: Put highest-scored chunks first in the prompt context. Models attend better to earlier content.
rule.rationale: LLMs exhibit "lost in the middle" behavior: facts in the middle of a long context are attended to less than facts at the beginning or end. Highest-relevance chunks should be first.

rule.statement: Instruct the model to answer ONLY from the provided context. Include an explicit fallback instruction: "If the context does not contain the answer, say so."
rule.rationale: Without this constraint, the model falls back to its training knowledge and produces answers that appear grounded but are not. The fallback instruction prevents confident hallucination.

rule.statement: Always request citations in a consistent format: [Source: filename, page N]. Parse and validate them against retrieved chunk metadata post-generation.
rule.rationale: Citations without validation are unverified assertions. The model may cite a plausible-sounding source that was not actually retrieved. Validation against chunk metadata catches hallucinated citations.

rule.statement: Run a faithfulness evaluator on production responses. When faithfulness score is below 0.8, queue for human review rather than surfacing to users.
rule.rationale: Low faithfulness means the generated answer contains claims not supported by the retrieved context. These are hallucinations that can damage user trust and cause compliance failures.

rule.statement: Evaluate retrieval quality with MRR greater than 0.7 and faithfulness greater than 0.9 before launching a RAG pipeline to production.
rule.rationale: MRR below 0.7 means the most relevant document is often not in the top-3 retrieved results — the generated answer is likely wrong or incomplete. Faithfulness below 0.9 means 10%+ of answers contain hallucinated claims.

rule.statement: Track retrieval quality with regular benchmarks on a held-out query set. Re-evaluate after any index, chunking, or embedding model change.
rule.rationale: Index updates, embedding model upgrades, and chunking parameter changes all affect retrieval quality. Without re-evaluation, quality regressions go undetected until user complaints surface them.

rule.statement: When a query requires combining facts from more than 3 separate chunks, switch to iterative (agentic) RAG rather than single-shot retrieval.
rule.rationale: Standard RAG degrades on multi-hop reasoning because a single retrieval step cannot surface all relevant chunks for complex cross-document questions. Iterative RAG fetches additional context based on intermediate answers.

rule.statement: Recommended subagents: query-embedder (embeds user query and calls vector store API), reranker (scores retrieved chunks with cross-encoder; returns top N), rag-generator (synthesizes answer from reranked context; enforces "only use provided context" constraint), citation-extractor (parses source references and validates against chunk metadata).
rule.rationale: Separating concerns enables independent testing, error recovery, and reuse of each subagent in different RAG variants.

rule.statement: Reference implementations use: llama-index (end-to-end RAG), langchain (RAG chains, retrievers, document loaders), ragas (RAG evaluation: faithfulness, answer relevance, context recall), qdrant-client (Python client for Qdrant), unstructured (parse PDFs, DOCX, HTML to clean text).
rule.rationale: These tools provide the standard interfaces for building production RAG systems with well-supported APIs.

rule.statement: Vector database selection: Qdrant (recommended production self-hosted), Chroma (dev/prototyping), LlamaCloud (managed parsing + indexing), Pinecone (fully managed; serverless tier), plus reranking services (Cohere Rerank: SaaS best-in-class, OpenAI Embeddings: text-embedding-3-large standard choice, Voyage AI: text-voyage-3 alternative).
rule.rationale: Qdrant provides the best balance of performance, filtering capabilities, and self-hosting flexibility. Cohere and OpenAI embeddings are the industry standards for quality and availability.

rule.statement: Test chunk size empirically: 512 tokens for Q&A, 1024 for technical docs, 1500 for research papers. Always add metadata (source filename, page, section, date) at index time — it's impossible to add retroactively without re-indexing.
rule.rationale: Chunk size dramatically affects both retrieval and generation quality. Metadata is the foundation for citations and filtered retrieval.

rule.statement: Use hybrid search (BM25 + vector) by default; fall back to pure vector only after benchmarking shows no improvement. Evaluate retrieval quality with RAGAS before launching; aim for MRR greater than 0.7, faithfulness greater than 0.9.
rule.rationale: Hybrid search catches exact terms that pure semantic search misses. RAGAS metrics provide quantitative gates before production launch.

rule.statement: Cache frequent queries at the application layer — same query string → same retrieval result. Implement incremental indexing: track document hashes, re-index only changed documents. Deduplicate near-identical chunks before indexing (cosine similarity greater than 0.97) to avoid redundant context.
rule.rationale: Caching eliminates redundant API calls. Incremental indexing scales to large corpora. Deduplication reduces token waste and improves retrieval precision.

rule.statement: Retrieved context order matters: put highest-scored chunks first in the prompt (models attend better to earlier content). Agents may silently ignore "answer only from context" constraints — add an explicit faithfulness check as a post-generation step.
rule.rationale: LLM attention patterns favor earlier content. Post-checks catch hallucination that the system prompt alone cannot prevent.

rule.statement: If the query requires combining facts from more than 3 separate chunks, standard RAG degrades; switch to agentic RAG with iterative retrieval. Index re-embedding after changing the embedding model requires complete re-indexing — plan for downtime or blue/green index strategy.
rule.rationale: Multi-hop reasoning needs multiple retrieval steps. Embedding model changes are high-risk operational events requiring careful planning.

rule.statement: Token budget: top_k * avg_chunk_tokens must stay well below the model's context limit after adding system prompt and few-shot examples. Human-in-loop checkpoint: when faithfulness score less than 0.8 on production queries, queue them for human review rather than surfacing the answer to users.
rule.rationale: Context overflow causes the model to drop earlier chunks. Human review gates catch low-confidence answers before they harm user trust.
